trans-unit.source: Good Map Design & Semiological Errors
trans-unit.target: Good Map Design & Semiological Errors

trans-unit.source: In this chapter we will discuss well designed maps and give examples of how to recreate specific design elements in QGIS. A second part of this chapter will focus on common semiological missteps. If you need further examples for good map design, check out these websites/repositories:
trans-unit.target: In this chapter we will discuss well designed maps and give examples of how to recreate specific design elements in QGIS. A second part of this chapter will focus on common semiological missteps. If you need further examples for good map design, check out these websites/repositories:

trans-unit.source: [impact-initiatives.org/resource-centre maps](https://www.impact-initiatives.org/resource-centre/?category%5B%5D=information_products&category%5B%5D=data_methods&type%5B%5D=281&order=latest&limit=10)
trans-unit.target: [impact-initiatives.org/resource-centre maps](https://www.impact-initiatives.org/resource-centre/?category%5B%5D=information_products&category%5B%5D=data_methods&type%5B%5D=281&order=latest&limit=10)

trans-unit.source: [geo.msf.org maps](https://geo.msf.org/catalogue/DOCID-1877329211-4979?from=0&sort=_score&desc=true)
trans-unit.target: [geo.msf.org maps](https://geo.msf.org/catalogue/DOCID-1877329211-4979?from=0&sort=_score&desc=true)

trans-unit.source: [reliefweb.int maps](https://reliefweb.int/updates?list=Maps%20/%20Infographics&view=maps)
trans-unit.target: [reliefweb.int maps](https://reliefweb.int/updates?list=Maps%20/%20Infographics&view=maps)

trans-unit.source: Map Examples
trans-unit.target: Map Examples

trans-unit.source: Map Example 1: Flood-affected areas and roads in the Somali Region, Ethiopia
trans-unit.target: Map Example 1: Flood-affected areas and roads in the Somali Region, Ethiopia

source.ph: name: Flood affected Areas in Somali

target.ph: name: Flood affected Areas in Somali

trans-unit.source: Flood affected areas and roads in the Somali Region, Ethiopia (Source: OCHA). :::
trans-unit.target: Flood affected areas and roads in the Somali Region, Ethiopia (Source: OCHA). :::

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Context: Situation in Ethiopia
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Context: Situation in Ethiopia

trans-unit.source: The Greater Horn of Africa receives 20 to 70 percent of the annual total rainfall in the months from October to December. The IFRC reports an exceptionally high forecast probability (over 80%) of experiencing wetter than normal rainfall conditions. Additionally, El Niño conditions started between July and August, which further accrued the possibility of high rainfall conditions in Ethiopia.
trans-unit.target: The Greater Horn of Africa receives 20 to 70 percent of the annual total rainfall in the months from October to December. The IFRC reports an exceptionally high forecast probability (over 80%) of experiencing wetter than normal rainfall conditions. Additionally, El Niño conditions started between July and August, which further accrued the possibility of high rainfall conditions in Ethiopia.

trans-unit.source: Since October, floods have affected at least 763,000 people in the region, taken 33 lives in the Somali Region alone, and killed 4,806 livestock. The flooding has also resulted in immense damage to infrastructure, transportation, and schooling. The population's livelihood and health have been greatly affected. The impact of flooding is projected to increase in the next few years, which will lead to more flash and river floods.
trans-unit.target: Since October, floods have affected at least 763,000 people in the region, taken 33 lives in the Somali Region alone, and killed 4,806 livestock. The flooding has also resulted in immense damage to infrastructure, transportation, and schooling. The population's livelihood and health have been greatly affected. The impact of flooding is projected to increase in the next few years, which will lead to more flash and river floods.

trans-unit.source: Access maps, such as the one above, play a crucial role in helping inform information managers and ground staff which areas are accessible. This is especially important as a timely deployment of relief or aid operations is essential in flooding disasters.
trans-unit.target: Access maps, such as the one above, play a crucial role in helping inform information managers and ground staff which areas are accessible. This is especially important as a timely deployment of relief or aid operations is essential in flooding disasters.

trans-unit.source: (Source: [IFRC](https://go.ifrc.org/emergencies/6773/details)) :::
trans-unit.target: (Source: [IFRC](https://go.ifrc.org/emergencies/6773/details)) :::

trans-unit.source: The map above shows the flood-affected areas, as well as the important road networks in the Somali Region, Ethiopia in November 2023. Maps such as these are crucial for humanitarian aid workers when planning relief or aid operations and need to be up-to-date. They display important settlements and, most importantly, the accessibility of roads and airstrips.
trans-unit.target: The map above shows the flood-affected areas, as well as the important road networks in the Somali Region, Ethiopia in November 2023. Maps such as these are crucial for humanitarian aid workers when planning relief or aid operations and need to be up-to-date. They display important settlements and, most importantly, the accessibility of roads and airstrips.

trans-unit.source: This is a thematic map with a clear purpose, featuring only the most essential elements relevant to that purpose.
trans-unit.target: This is a thematic map with a clear purpose, featuring only the most essential elements relevant to that purpose.

trans-unit.source: A shapefile for the flood affected areas was given a hashed fill. In QGIS, you can find this symbology.
trans-unit.target: A shapefile for the flood affected areas was given a hashed fill. In QGIS, you can find this symbology.

trans-unit.source: A layer with the road network has been put above the layer with the flood-affected areas. The road symbology has been __categorised__ into three categories: Accessible road (green), partially accessible road (grey), and hard-to-reach road (red).
trans-unit.target: A layer with the road network has been put above the layer with the flood-affected areas. The road symbology has been __categorised__ into three categories: Accessible road (green), partially accessible road (grey), and hard-to-reach road (red).

trans-unit.source: The topmost layer is a point-layer with information on inaccessible roads or bridges as well as the location of airstrips and which airstrips are accessible. The points have been symbolized with SVG-symbols.
trans-unit.target: The topmost layer is a point-layer with information on inaccessible roads or bridges as well as the location of airstrips and which airstrips are accessible. The points have been symbolized with SVG-symbols.

trans-unit.source: (The administrative boundaries of Ethiopia are set apart from the surrounding countries by making the polygon a clear white and the surrounding countries in a shade of grey. This can be achieved by copying the polygon of Ethiopia into a new layer, and changing the symbology respectively).
trans-unit.target: (The administrative boundaries of Ethiopia are set apart from the surrounding countries by making the polygon a clear white and the surrounding countries in a shade of grey. This can be achieved by copying the polygon of Ethiopia into a new layer, and changing the symbology respectively).

trans-unit.source: Map Example 2: Flooding Risk in the Ouham Region, Central African Republic
trans-unit.target: Map Example 2: Flooding Risk in the Ouham Region, Central African Republic

source.ph: name: REACH Flooding Risk Ouhman Region, Central African Republic

target.ph: name: REACH Flooding Risk Ouhman Region, Central African Republic

trans-unit.source: Flooding risk in the Ouham Region, Central African Republic (Source: REACH). :::
trans-unit.target: Flooding risk in the Ouham Region, Central African Republic (Source: REACH). :::

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Context: Situation in the Central African Republic
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Context: Situation in the Central African Republic

trans-unit.source: The Central African Republic has been hit by destructive floods in late 2019, which displaced over 100,000 people and caused considerable damage to infrastructure. The floods have destroyed shelters, obstructed transportation routes, and have led to disease outbreaks such as cholera and malaria. Due to climate change, such floods will become more frequent, leading to increased vulnerability for towns and villages. Since natural hazards are hard to predict, the changing climate reduces community resilience.
trans-unit.target: The Central African Republic has been hit by destructive floods in late 2019, which displaced over 100,000 people and caused considerable damage to infrastructure. The floods have destroyed shelters, obstructed transportation routes, and have led to disease outbreaks such as cholera and malaria. Due to climate change, such floods will become more frequent, leading to increased vulnerability for towns and villages. Since natural hazards are hard to predict, the changing climate reduces community resilience.

trans-unit.source: Source: [REACH Initiative](https://reliefweb.int/report/central-african-republic/central-african-republic-flood-susceptibility-risk) :::
trans-unit.target: Source: [REACH Initiative](https://reliefweb.int/report/central-african-republic/central-african-republic-flood-susceptibility-risk) :::

trans-unit.source: This map displays the flooding risk using a raster image. The raster data was calculated using several factors, including the precipitation intensity, the maximum duration of precipitation, the height of the nearest drainage, the flow direction and river network, the topographic humidity, a digital elevation model, and the ground cover.
trans-unit.target: This map displays the flooding risk using a raster image. The raster data was calculated using several factors, including the precipitation intensity, the maximum duration of precipitation, the height of the nearest drainage, the flow direction and river network, the topographic humidity, a digital elevation model, and the ground cover.

trans-unit.source: The raster data is displayed using a diverging colour ramp. (Here you can see how to assign a colour ramp).
trans-unit.target: The raster data is displayed using a diverging colour ramp. (Here you can see how to assign a colour ramp).

trans-unit.source: The surrounding administrative districts have been overlayed with a transparent grey.
trans-unit.target: The surrounding administrative districts have been overlayed with a transparent grey.

trans-unit.source: The river network has been added in blue.
trans-unit.target: The river network has been added in blue.

trans-unit.source: The main roads as well have been added in black.
trans-unit.target: The main roads as well have been added in black.

trans-unit.source: Settlements are displayed as black dots. This helps to identify areas with a higher population density in the areas most at risk.
trans-unit.target: Settlements are displayed as black dots. This helps to identify areas with a higher population density in the areas most at risk.

trans-unit.source: Common Missteps in Semiology
trans-unit.target: Common Missteps in Semiology

trans-unit.source: The Modifiable Areal Unit Problem
trans-unit.target: The Modifiable Areal Unit Problem

trans-unit.source: The Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP) is a statistical bias that arises when spatial data is aggregated into regions. It highlights how the results of spatial analysis can change depending on how data is grouped into areal units (spatial zones).
trans-unit.target: The Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP) is a statistical bias that arises when spatial data is aggregated into regions. It highlights how the results of spatial analysis can change depending on how data is grouped into areal units (spatial zones).

trans-unit.source: The Modifiable Areal Unit Problem has two key components:
trans-unit.target: The Modifiable Areal Unit Problem has two key components:

trans-unit.source: __Scale Effect:__
trans-unit.target: __Scale Effect:__

trans-unit.source: The scale of aggregation (small vs. large areas) affects the results.
trans-unit.target: The scale of aggregation (small vs. large areas) affects the results.

trans-unit.source: When smaller units (e.g., census blocks) are used, the analysis may capture fine-grained local variations.
trans-unit.target: When smaller units (e.g., census blocks) are used, the analysis may capture fine-grained local variations.

trans-unit.source: When larger units (e.g., counties or states) are used, local variations get smoothed out, and the results may show broader trends. For example, average income might vary significantly at the neighborhood level but look more uniform at the county level.
trans-unit.target: When larger units (e.g., counties or states) are used, local variations get smoothed out, and the results may show broader trends. For example, average income might vary significantly at the neighborhood level but look more uniform at the county level.

trans-unit.source: __Zoning Effect:__
trans-unit.target: __Zoning Effect:__

trans-unit.source: The shape and arrangement of the zones used for aggregation can also affect the results.
trans-unit.target: The shape and arrangement of the zones used for aggregation can also affect the results.

trans-unit.source: Changing the boundaries of zones (e.g., splitting a city into east-west vs. north-south regions) can lead to different outcomes, even if the total population or data remains the same. This happens because boundaries influence how values are averaged or summed.
trans-unit.target: Changing the boundaries of zones (e.g., splitting a city into east-west vs. north-south regions) can lead to different outcomes, even if the total population or data remains the same. This happens because boundaries influence how values are averaged or summed.

trans-unit.source: __Why is this important in GIS?__
trans-unit.target: __Why is this important in GIS?__

trans-unit.source: Policy Decisions: If the analysis depends on arbitrary boundaries, decisions (e.g., allocating resources) might be based on misleading results.
trans-unit.target: Policy Decisions: If the analysis depends on arbitrary boundaries, decisions (e.g., allocating resources) might be based on misleading results.

trans-unit.source: Spatial Statistics: Correlations, regressions, and other analyses involving spatial data can be biased due to MAUP.
trans-unit.target: Spatial Statistics: Correlations, regressions, and other analyses involving spatial data can be biased due to MAUP.

trans-unit.source: Visualising the Modifiable Areal Unit Problem: The same indicator represented at three different scales (Source: Kitchin, Rob & Lauriault, Tracey & McArdle, Gavin. (2015). Knowing and governing cities through urban indicators, city benchmarking and real-time dashboards. Regional Studies, Regional Science. 2. 6-28. 10.1080/21681376.2014.983149.). :::
trans-unit.target: Visualising the Modifiable Areal Unit Problem: The same indicator represented at three different scales (Source: Kitchin, Rob & Lauriault, Tracey & McArdle, Gavin. (2015). Knowing and governing cities through urban indicators, city benchmarking and real-time dashboards. Regional Studies, Regional Science. 2. 6-28. 10.1080/21681376.2014.983149.). :::

trans-unit.source: Proportional Circles vs. Solid Colours
trans-unit.target: Proportional Circles vs. Solid Colours

trans-unit.source: While it is graphically appealing, representing quantitative data with solid colours can lead to problems and distract from the message of the map:
trans-unit.target: While it is graphically appealing, representing quantitative data with solid colours can lead to problems and distract from the message of the map:

trans-unit.source: You lose the __order relationship between the data__ (a circle can be twice as big as another one, a colour cannot be "twice as dark").
trans-unit.target: You lose the __order relationship between the data__ (a circle can be twice as big as another one, a colour cannot be "twice as dark").

trans-unit.source: Countries with a large surface area stand out visually (e.g. Russia in the example below).
trans-unit.target: Countries with a large surface area stand out visually (e.g. Russia in the example below).

trans-unit.source: We are trying to represent __data that has nothing to do with the area of a country__.
trans-unit.target: We are trying to represent __data that has nothing to do with the area of a country__.

trans-unit.source: Colour Gradient vs. Distinct Colour Palette
trans-unit.target: Colour Gradient vs. Distinct Colour Palette

trans-unit.source: A representation that "feels right" because it seems logical that a "low" rate would be represented differently than a "high" rate.
trans-unit.target: A representation that "feels right" because it seems logical that a "low" rate would be represented differently than a "high" rate.

trans-unit.source: It is a mistake because:
trans-unit.target: It is a mistake because:

trans-unit.source: By using a differentiating colour variable, __you lose the ordinal relationship between entities__. Instead, a __gradient of the same colour__ that should be used.
trans-unit.target: By using a differentiating colour variable, __you lose the ordinal relationship between entities__. Instead, a __gradient of the same colour__ that should be used.

trans-unit.source: Different colours are used to differentiate between distinct entities.
trans-unit.target: Different colours are used to differentiate between distinct entities.

trans-unit.source: Gradient in a Single Colour vs. Gradient between two Colours
trans-unit.target: Gradient in a Single Colour vs. Gradient between two Colours

trans-unit.source: This is difficult because our brains are used to prioritising certain colours, especially green to red, or blue to red. We must remember that __if our values do not have a meaningful zero point__, it might be better to stay in the same single colour and use different shades of that colour to indicate different values. Alternatively, a colour gradient that is not diverging can be used.
trans-unit.target: This is difficult because our brains are used to prioritising certain colours, especially green to red, or blue to red. We must remember that __if our values do not have a meaningful zero point__, it might be better to stay in the same single colour and use different shades of that colour to indicate different values. Alternatively, a colour gradient that is not diverging can be used.

trans-unit.source: A diverging gradient between two colours can be used when it is necessary to show a gradation that can go from negative to positive. As for temperatures, it makes sense to distinguish negative values (in shades of blue for example) and positive values (in shades of red).
trans-unit.target: A diverging gradient between two colours can be used when it is necessary to show a gradation that can go from negative to positive. As for temperatures, it makes sense to distinguish negative values (in shades of blue for example) and positive values (in shades of red).

trans-unit.source: It is a mistake because:
trans-unit.target: It is a mistake because:

trans-unit.source: By choosing different colours for values that are linked to each other, our eyes perceive a difference between the elements, and not an order.
trans-unit.target: By choosing different colours for values that are linked to each other, our eyes perceive a difference between the elements, and not an order.

trans-unit.source: Darker colours stand out more than lighter colours, and can be perceived as more important.
trans-unit.target: Darker colours stand out more than lighter colours, and can be perceived as more important.

trans-unit.source: The map will send a message of divergence, of opposition between certain values, when we are simply trying to represent a hierarchy between values.
trans-unit.target: The map will send a message of divergence, of opposition between certain values, when we are simply trying to represent a hierarchy between values.

trans-unit.source: In this way, the colour itself directly indicates information about the trend (positive/negative or increasing/decreasing).
trans-unit.target: In this way, the colour itself directly indicates information about the trend (positive/negative or increasing/decreasing).

trans-unit.source: Limited Geometric Symbols vs. Complex Icons and Symbols
trans-unit.target: Limited Geometric Symbols vs. Complex Icons and Symbols

trans-unit.source: Incorporating a multitude of symbols (and data) for an informative map is a common desire. However, too many symbols can __overload the map__ and __reduce the readability__ of the map. Using too many symbols (especially geometric ones) can make it difficult to read and understand the map. __The eye can easily distinguish between four to five different symbols.__ Beyond that, it is difficult to tell elements apart. However, this is a less serious error because it does not convey false information on the map.
trans-unit.target: Incorporating a multitude of symbols (and data) for an informative map is a common desire. However, too many symbols can __overload the map__ and __reduce the readability__ of the map. Using too many symbols (especially geometric ones) can make it difficult to read and understand the map. __The eye can easily distinguish between four to five different symbols.__ Beyond that, it is difficult to tell elements apart. However, this is a less serious error because it does not convey false information on the map.

trans-unit.source: It is a mistake because:
trans-unit.target: It is a mistake because:

trans-unit.source: It complicates the map and limits its impact.
trans-unit.target: It complicates the map and limits its impact.

trans-unit.source: Sometimes you are forced to represent several symbols, so you must be careful about overlapping points and overloading the map.
trans-unit.target: Sometimes you are forced to represent several symbols, so you must be careful about overlapping points and overloading the map.

trans-unit.source: Self-Assessment Questions
trans-unit.target: Self-Assessment Questions

trans-unit.source: ::::{admonition} Test your knowledge :class: note
trans-unit.target: ::::{admonition} Test your knowledge :class: note

trans-unit.source: __What makes a map “good” or effective? In your own words, list at least three qualities or principles illustrated by the example maps.__
trans-unit.target: __What makes a map “good” or effective? In your own words, list at least three qualities or principles illustrated by the example maps.__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: __Clarity/readability at a glance__
trans-unit.target: __Clarity/readability at a glance__

trans-unit.source: A good map allows the viewer to immediately understand the key message or distribution without having to decipher overly complex symbology. For example, the example maps use clean colour schemes (or minimal distractions) so that the spatial pattern stands out.
trans-unit.target: A good map allows the viewer to immediately understand the key message or distribution without having to decipher overly complex symbology. For example, the example maps use clean colour schemes (or minimal distractions) so that the spatial pattern stands out.

trans-unit.source: __Appropriate visual hierarchy and focus__
trans-unit.target: __Appropriate visual hierarchy and focus__

trans-unit.source: That means emphasising the main theme or data of the map, while de-emphasising background or context layers so they don’t compete. For example, context features (roads, boundaries, basemap) are often light, subtle, or greyed out; while the thematic layers are bold. This directs the viewer’s eye to what matters. One of the example maps shows how subsidiary layers are subdued so the main data pops out.
trans-unit.target: That means emphasising the main theme or data of the map, while de-emphasising background or context layers so they don’t compete. For example, context features (roads, boundaries, basemap) are often light, subtle, or greyed out; while the thematic layers are bold. This directs the viewer’s eye to what matters. One of the example maps shows how subsidiary layers are subdued so the main data pops out.

trans-unit.source: __Fit for purpose/audience and effective design choices__
trans-unit.target: __Fit for purpose/audience and effective design choices__

trans-unit.source: A good map aligns its design (colours, symbols, labelling, orientation) with the intended audience and the purpose (operational, humanitarian, public communication, scientific). For example, using intuitive colours (e.g., red for high risk, green for safe) or simplified icons for non‑technical audiences. The module implicitly shows different example map types tailored to humanitarian use, operational decision‑making, etc. :::
trans-unit.target: A good map aligns its design (colours, symbols, labelling, orientation) with the intended audience and the purpose (operational, humanitarian, public communication, scientific). For example, using intuitive colours (e.g., red for high risk, green for safe) or simplified icons for non‑technical audiences. The module implicitly shows different example map types tailored to humanitarian use, operational decision‑making, etc. :::

trans-unit.source: __Consider the intended audience and use case for one of the example maps (e.g. humanitarian, operational, public communication). How do design choices reflect that audience (e.g. simplicity, clarity, icon selection)?__
trans-unit.target: __Consider the intended audience and use case for one of the example maps (e.g. humanitarian, operational, public communication). How do design choices reflect that audience (e.g. simplicity, clarity, icon selection)?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer Design choices should reflect that:
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer Design choices should reflect that:

trans-unit.source: __Simplicity and clarity__: The map avoids overly detailed or decorative features; it focuses on the essential: the affected area, safe zones/routes, relief hubs in the context of a flood-response map.
trans-unit.target: __Simplicity and clarity__: The map avoids overly detailed or decorative features; it focuses on the essential: the affected area, safe zones/routes, relief hubs in the context of a flood-response map.

trans-unit.source: __Use of intuitive icons/symbology__: For instance, a shelter icon, or a triangle for hazard, arrows for movement — easily understood without needing deep legend lookup.
trans-unit.target: __Use of intuitive icons/symbology__: For instance, a shelter icon, or a triangle for hazard, arrows for movement — easily understood without needing deep legend lookup.

trans-unit.source: __High contrast and meaningul colours__: For instance, red/orange for hazardous zones, green for safe or cleared areas, possibly a neutral grey for context. This helps non‑technical users quickly interpret what’s urgent.
trans-unit.target: __High contrast and meaningul colours__: For instance, red/orange for hazardous zones, green for safe or cleared areas, possibly a neutral grey for context. This helps non‑technical users quickly interpret what’s urgent.

trans-unit.source: __Minimal distractions__: The background may be subdued, roads/boundaries muted, to make the operational layer stand out. Also large readable fonts for titles/labels because maybe viewed in the field.
trans-unit.target: __Minimal distractions__: The background may be subdued, roads/boundaries muted, to make the operational layer stand out. Also large readable fonts for titles/labels because maybe viewed in the field.

trans-unit.source: __Clear map elements and layout for quick reference__: Big title (e.g., “Flood Damage Zones – 24 Oct 2025”), a prominent legend, scale bar, north arrow, and possibly an inset map for orientation. Thus, the design choices reflect the audience by prioritising readability, immediacy, and intuitive interpretability over cartographic elegance or deep detail. :::
trans-unit.target: __Clear map elements and layout for quick reference__: Big title (e.g., “Flood Damage Zones – 24 Oct 2025”), a prominent legend, scale bar, north arrow, and possibly an inset map for orientation. Thus, the design choices reflect the audience by prioritising readability, immediacy, and intuitive interpretability over cartographic elegance or deep detail. :::
